planting.note: Direct sow stratified seeds in spring in well-drained, sandy, acidic, poor soil in full sun; Virginia pine colonizes dry, infertile, disturbed sites naturally and establishes readily from seed. It is one of the most important pines for wildlife plantings on poor soils in the mid-Atlantic and upper South.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, poor, acidic soil in full sun; Virginia pine is a pioneer species of the mid-Atlantic and upper South, naturally colonizing dry, infertile, disturbed, and eroded sites where richer soils and better drainage favor other trees. It is a relatively small, irregularly shaped pine with short, twisted needles in pairs and persistent small cones that remain on the tree for years, providing seeds and cover for wildlife through winter. The tree's value for wildlife planting on poor soils is exceptional — it is a primary host for numerous moth and butterfly caterpillars, and the cone seeds are eaten by finches, chickadees, and other birds. Used extensively in reclamation planting on strip-mined land and abandoned agricultural soils in Appalachia and the Piedmont. Though not a timber tree, it serves as an important nurse tree that prepares infertile sites for succession to mixed hardwood forest.

planting.note: Direct sow stratified seeds in spring in very well-drained, rocky, acidic, lean soil in full sun; table mountain pine is strictly adapted to dry, rocky, high-elevation habitats and performs poorly in rich, moist, or lowland conditions. Plant on exposed, sunny slopes or rocky outcrops in the Appalachian region.

directions: Plant in dry, rocky, very well-drained, acidic, lean soil in full sun on exposed ridges and slopes; table mountain pine is an Appalachian endemic restricted to dry, exposed, rocky ridge tops from Pennsylvania to Georgia, typically growing on south- or west-facing outcrops at elevations of 2000–4000 feet. The most distinctive feature is the armored cones — among the most ferociously spiny of any North American pine, with stout, curved, sharp spines on each scale. These serotinous cones remain closed on the tree for decades and require the intense heat of a wildfire to open and release seeds, an adaptation to the fire-maintained pine barrens community of dry Appalachian ridges. The tree has a gnarled, picturesque growth habit from constant exposure to wind and drought on rocky summits. Of limited garden use outside its native range but outstanding for naturalistic plantings on rocky, thin, dry, acidic slopes in Appalachian zones 4–7.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to average, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to shade; Swan's sedge grows naturally in moist to dry woodland understory and meadow edges throughout eastern North America. Division of established clumps in spring is the most reliable propagation method.

directions: Plant in moist to average, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full shade; Swan's sedge is a common and adaptable woodland sedge of eastern North America, forming neat, arching clumps of medium-width, bright green leaves that remain attractive through the growing season. Unlike some native sedges adapted to wet or strictly dry conditions, Swan's sedge thrives across a broad range of soil moisture from average to moderately moist woodland soil, making it one of the more garden-adaptable woodland natives. The nodding flower spikes in late spring are unremarkable but the foliage is clean and attractive as a mid-height groundcover or clump accent in the shade garden. Naturalizes readily in shaded areas and combines well with ferns, native gingers, and woodland wildflowers in the layered shade garden.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to dry, poor to average, acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; path rush is one of the most adaptable and commonly overlooked native rushes, growing naturally along compacted paths, in disturbed areas, woodland edges, and open understory throughout eastern North America.

directions: Plant in moist to average, poor to average, acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; path rush is one of the most ubiquitous and overlooked native rushes, a slender, wiry plant of paths, roadsides, woodland edges, and compacted soils throughout eastern North America. It is named for its habit of growing along beaten paths and in disturbed areas where compacted, nutrient-poor soil discourages competition. The very fine, wiry, dark green culms are cylindrical and leafless in the true rush manner, producing small, compact clusters of flowers and seeds near mid-culm. While not showy, path rush is an excellent native filler for difficult, shaded, or compacted areas where other plants struggle. It self-sows freely in appropriate conditions and provides fine-textured contrast to broader-leaved shade plants. The seed heads are eaten by sparrows and other small birds. A reliable, low-maintenance component of ecological plantings in disturbed, shaded areas.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun to partial shade; whitetop sedge grows naturally in wet pine savannas, bog margins, and seepage wetlands of the Coastal Plain from North Carolina to Texas. It requires consistently moist to wet, acidic, low-nutrient soil.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty, nutrient-poor soil in full sun to partial shade; whitetop sedge is one of the most ornamentally striking native wetland sedges of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, creating a spectacular display in late spring and summer when the broad, white-tipped bracts (modified leaves) surrounding the flower heads turn brilliant white — creating the illusion that the plant is covered in large, pure white flowers. The white coloration comes from the dramatically broadened, leaf-like bracts that subtend the small true flowers, a feature unique to the genus Rhynchospora. In naturalistic wet gardens, rain gardens, pond margins, and bog plantings in zones 7–10, whitetop sedge provides exceptional ornamental interest that no other native plant can match. The white display lasts for weeks and brightens moist, shaded areas dramatically. Requires consistently wet, acidic, low-nutrient soil similar to a pine flatwood.
